answer:
Iqbal's *Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam* ("An Address to the Muslim Youth") adopts a poetic, exhortative posture toward the youth of Islam, shaming their current inertia ("you are all talk, they were all action; you are static, they were ever-moving") while evoking ancestral glory—the desert Arabs who built empires through proud poverty and divine spirit—to inspire revival and action amid lost heritage (e.g., forefathers' books now in Europe).[^1] This rhetoric shapes the addressee as a "shattered star" needing reconnection to cosmic origins, prioritizing spiritual dynamism over lamenting lost rule.[^2]

In *Sach keh doon ae Brahman!* ("O Brahmin, if you will not take offense"), Iqbal shifts to candid, dialogic critique of a Hindu priestly figure, bluntly declaring idols "have lost reverence" and urging transcendence of divisive idols/mosques for a unified "city of the heart" via universal love and homeland divinity ("To me, my homeland's every speck is divine").[^3] The posture is provocatively irenic, positioning the Brahmin as a potential partner in erasing "veils of otherness," fostering shared humanism over ritual scorn.[^4]

Manu Chapter 7 declares the "Dharma of Kings," imperatively instructing the ruler—divinely synthesized from gods' particles—as protector wielding *daṇḍa* (punishment) to enforce *varṇāśrama-dharma*, detailing routines (e.g., consulting Vedic Brahmins, avoiding vices like hunting/gambling), ministers, forts, and gifts to Brahmins.[^5] The rhetorical posture is hieratic and exhaustive, shaping the king as a "great deity in human form" whose just severity ensures cosmic order, subordinating personal vices to varna protection.[^6]

Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation* (Vol. I), a philosophical treatise, implicitly addresses legislators/students via analytical exposition of law's "elements of force" (physical/moral pains/pleasures, opinions), critiquing flawed systems (e.g., divine will, utility without facts) for an empirical science grounded in human nature.[^7] Its preface explicitly targets "young people" studying legislation, advocating methodical observation over partisan speculation to explain (not prescribe) laws' genesis/effects.[^8] The posture is didactic-scientific, shaping addressees as impartial analysts reconciling theory/practice, unlike impulsive "men of systems."[^9]

**Comparison**: All employ "addressee-shaping" via direct invocation (poetic in Iqbal, normative in Manu/Comte), but differ in tone and telos. Iqbal's dual postures are inspirational/dialogic, mobilizing youth via shame/pride and Brahmin via bold unity-call, emphasizing subjective revival over rules. Manu's is theocratic-didactic, binding the king to ritual hierarchy for social stasis. Comte's is secular-analytic, equipping legislators with causal insight for progressive reform, prioritizing method over divine/moral fiat. Iqbal personalizes spiritual agency; Manu/Comte systematize duty/rule-making, with Manu's divine absolutism contrasting Comte's empirical humanism.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.13 ("Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam"), [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462).
[^2]: Ibid., [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-0291d8).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch.36 ("Sach keh doon ae Brahman!"), [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/36-sach-keh-doon-ae-brahman-gar-tu-bura-na-maane/translation/#p-752d60).
[^4]: Ibid., [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/36-sach-keh-doon-ae-brahman-gar-tu-bura-na-maane/translation/#p-db56fb).
[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, ch.7, [translation/](works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-b46e87).
[^6]: Ibid., [translation/](works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-915921).
[^7]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, [work wiki](works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/wiki/_work.card.md).
[^8]: Ibid., ch.22 ("Préface"), [translation/](works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/22-preface/translation/#p-a65ce7).
[^9]: Ibid., [translation/](works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/22-preface/translation/#p-520df4).
